task: I’ve got a batch review where a couple of units look off, and I need to record what I found before anything gets moved forward. What’s a practical way to note the details, keep any photos or paperwork organized, and leave enough information for the next person to understand the decision?

turns.agent_response:
Use a simple **decision log + hold/accept set-aside** workflow so nothing gets moved forward on a gut call.

### 1) Sort the units first
For each unit, mark one of three statuses:
- **Accept** — matches paperwork/photos/measurements
- **Set Aside / Hold** — looks off or needs re-check
- **Formal Write-Up** — confirmed defect, mismatch, or repeated issue

### 2) Record each unit with a traceable log entry
Use a consistent line for every item:

- **Part/Unit ID:**
- **Lot/Batch #:**
- **Location on pallet/rack/box:**
- **What I observed:**  
  (example: “edge chip on lower left corner,” “label mismatch,” “dimension out by 0.8 mm”)
- **Measurement / photo reference:**  
  (include file name or photo number)
- **Paperwork reference:**  
  (PO, traveler, inspection sheet, etc.)
- **Status:** Accept / Hold / Write-Up
- **Next step:** re-measure, re-check label, verify with owner, quality review
- **Who checked / when:**

### 3) Keep photos and paperwork tied to the same physical unit
Make sure each photo and document can be linked to the exact item:
- Use the **same unit ID and lot number** in the photo name and log
- If a photo or note can’t be tied to the physical unit, **don’t sign off yet**
- Put questionable items in a **HOLD** group until verified

### 4) Use a short decision note for the next person
Write a clear note like:

> **HOLD:** Unit B-14 in Lot 23-771 shows label mismatch and edge damage. Photo 03 and measurement sheet 2 attached. Needs re-check against traveler before release.

### 5) Flag related items if there’s a pattern
If the issue seems tied to the same lot, setup, or station:
- note the **pattern**
- flag other units from the same source for follow-up
- mention whether they need **re-measure, re-check, or re-test**

### 6) Keep the record explicit
Avoid vague notes like “looks bad” or “needs review.”  
Use concrete details:
- exact part
- exact location
- what is different
- what evidence supports the call
- what happens next

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page batch review template** you can copy into your log.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. I’d want it a little tighter though — more like something you can use on the floor without thinking about it.

Set it up like this:

1. **Unit ID / lot / location**
2. **What’s off** — plain words, no guessing
3. **What I verified** — measurement, label, count, whatever
4. **Photo numbers / file names**
5. **Paperwork tied to it** — traveler, PO, inspection sheet
6. **Disposition** — hold, recheck, reject, accept
7. **Who knows / when** — initials, time, maybe supervisor if needed

For the next person, the note should answer three things fast:
- what was found
- why it was held or rejected
- what needs to happen before release

Something like:

**HOLD — Unit 7, Lot 24-118. Corner damage and label mismatch. Photo 2 and 3 attached. Measured 0.6 mm out on width. Recheck against traveler before release.**

That’s the kind of note I’d trust. If you want, I can lay it out as a clean batch log form with blanks you can fill in.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter **floor-use batch review format** that stays clear, traceable, and easy for the next person to follow.

## Batch review note format
Use one line or short block per unit:

**Unit ID / Lot / Location:**  
**What’s off:**  
**What I verified:**  
**Photo file # / name:**  
**Paperwork tied to it:**  
**Disposition:** Hold / Recheck / Reject / Accept  
**Who knows / when:** Initials, date, time, supervisor if needed  
**Next step / owner:**  

## What to write in each field
- **What’s off** = plain description only  
  - Example: *corner damage, label mismatch, count short, dimension high*
- **What I verified** = actual check result  
  - Example: *measured 0.6 mm over spec, label does not match traveler, count 9 vs 10*
- **Disposition** = one clear status  
  - **Hold** = stop and wait
  - **Recheck** = needs another verify
  - **Reject** = confirmed fail
  - **Accept** = matches and can move forward

## Short note example
**Unit ID / Lot / Location:** Unit 7 / Lot 24-118 / Rack B3  
**What’s off:** Corner damage and label mismatch  
**What I verified:** Width measured 0.6 mm over spec; label does not match traveler  
**Photo file # / name:** IMG_023, IMG_024  
**Paperwork tied to it:** Traveler TR-118, Inspection Sheet IS-07  
**Disposition:** Hold  
**Who knows / when:** AB / 05-01-26 / 14:20 / Supervisor notified  
**Next step / owner:** Recheck against traveler before release / QA

## Good wording rule
Keep it specific:
- say **“dimension high”** instead of “off”
- say **“surface damage”** instead of “suspect”
- say **“label mismatch”** instead of “looks wrong”

## One important control
Before anything moves forward, make sure the **paperwork part/lot/serial/qty matches the actual product**. If it doesn’t match, it stays on hold.

If you want, I can turn this into a **fill-in-the-blank log sheet** you can copy straight into a notebook or spreadsheet.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah — that’s usable.

The one I’d actually run with is this:

**Unit ID / Lot / Location:**
**What’s off:**
**What I verified:**
**Photo file # / name:**
**Paperwork tied to it:**
**Disposition:** Hold / Recheck / Reject / Accept
**Who knows / when:**
**Next step / owner:**

Keeps it tight. No fluff, no guessing.

And the short note format is right too:

**HOLD — Unit 7 / Lot 24-118 / Rack B3. Corner damage and label mismatch. Width measured 0.6 mm over spec. Photo IMG_023 and IMG_024 attached. Traveler TR-118 does not match. Recheck before release.**

That tells the next person what happened, why it stopped, and what needs to happen next. Good enough to trust on the floor.

Only thing I’d add, if the issue is serious or repeated, is a quick line for **pattern / same lot / same station**. That’s the part people skip and then wonder why the same problem keeps showing up.
